topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official letter lay on my desk, the ink stark against the cream paper. My stomach twisted into a tight, unpleasant knot as I reread the lines. The scholarship, the one I had poured sleepless nights and agonizing effort into securing, was not mine. It belonged to someone else entirely. A cold, hard feeling settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I slowly pushed the paper away, the sound of the slight scrape against the wood echoing loudly in the quiet room. I stood up, my movements stiff, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus quad. Every bright, hopeful face seemed to mock the hollowness that had just been revealed. I didn't bother to cry; the tears felt too weak for the scale of this betrayal.

[story 2]

"This is absurd," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I held the notification screen up to the light, my jaw tight enough to crack. The figures, the names—they all pointed away from me. It was a deliberate misdirection, a cruel joke played by the administration. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran down my spine, a reaction I immediately tried to suppress by clenching my fists until my knuckles were white. I turned from the computer, my gaze sweeping over the surrounding students, cataloging them with a sudden, sharp disdain. They were all so earnest, so oblivious to the layers of deceit beneath the surface. I let out a short, humorless laugh, a dry sound that scraped against my throat.

[story 3]

I reread the fine print, my eyes scanning the jargon until they blurred. The realization hit me not as a shock, but as a dull, pervasive weight pressing down on my shoulders. It was a calculated maneuver, a setup designed to humiliate. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped my lips, laced with a profound sense of being utterly duped. I picked up my pen, not to sign anything, but just to turn it over and over in my fingers, watching the plastic casing deform under my grip. The air in the room felt thin, inadequate. I didn't need to shout or throw anything; the silence radiating from me was far more potent, a quiet declaration of absolute dismissal toward the entire arrangement.
